burial.plot_notes: Green Mount Cemetery West Goshen Township Chester County per FA

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' honors the family's lineage through Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong — his great-great-grandmother — who married Elisha Beebe Strong in 1813 and introduced the Hooker name into the Strong family.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker in the fourth generation of Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker Strong was the fourth consecutive generation of the Strong family to carry 'Hooker' as a given name — following his father George Hooker Strong (born 1871), his grandfather George Parish Strong (born 1824), and ultimately tracing back to Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong who first planted the name in the Strong family through her 1813 marriage. His own son, Captain Henry Hooker Strong Jr., would carry it into a fifth generation and into the Vietnam War, where he was shot down over North Vietnam in 1972 at age thirty-nine.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

bio.title: Captain
bio.photo_notes: Captain Henry Hooker Strong Jr. (1933-1972), U.S. Navy Skyhawk pilot killed in action over North Vietnam

death.cause: Killed in action — A-4 Skyhawk shot down by enemy fire near Vinh City, North Vietnam

burial.plot_notes: Arlington National Cemetery — MIA/KIA; promoted to Captain while maintained as Missing in Action

notable.notable_blurb: Navy Skyhawk pilot shot down over North Vietnam

military_service.notes: Participated in strike mission near Vinh City, North Vietnam, May 25, 1972. Aircraft shot down by enemy fire. Declared Missing in Action. Promoted to Captain during period maintained as missing. Awards: Distinguished Flying Cross, Bronze Star Medal, Air Medal (Gold Star), Navy Commendation Medal with V4GS.

naming_inspiration.description: Fifth consecutive generation to carry 'Hooker' as a middle name in the Strong family, descending from Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong (H00795) through four generations to this Navy pilot who carried the name into Vietnam.

narrative_blocks.header: A Skyhawk pilot shot down near Vinh City
narrative_blocks.body: Captain Henry Hooker Strong Jr. flew the A-4 Skyhawk on a strike mission near Vinh City, North Vietnam on May 25, 1972, when his aircraft was shot down by enemy fire and he was declared Missing in Action — promoted to Captain during the period he was maintained as missing, a posthumous recognition accompanied by the Distinguished Flying Cross, Bronze Star Medal, and Air Medal. He was thirty-nine years old, a name from the fifth consecutive generation of Hooker-bearing Strongs that traced all the way back to Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong's 1813 marriage to Elisha Beebe Strong in Canandaigua, New York.

narrative_blocks.header: The fifth Hooker-named Strong in an unbroken line
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Hooker Strong Jr. was the fifth generation of the George Parish Strong branch of the family to carry 'Hooker' as a given name: Dolly Goodwin Hooker Strong (who married into the family), George Hooker Strong (her grandson), Henry Hooker Strong Sr. (George's son), and then Henry Hooker Strong Jr. — a chain of naming that began in 1871 with a birth in Kansas and ended at Arlington National Cemetery in the Vietnam era. His sister Judith Ann Strong married Charles Kinney, a fellow Naval Aviation Cadet of Henry's, completing the family's deep entanglement with the Navy.

sources.label: U.S. Navy records — Captain Henry Hooker Strong Jr., KIA May 25, 1972

burial.plot_notes: Arlington National Cemetery — beside her husband Charles Herbert Kinney

narrative_blocks.header: Married her brother's fellow Naval Aviation Cadet
narrative_blocks.body: Judith Ann Strong married Charles Herbert Kinney on July 16, 1960 in Virginia Beach, Virginia — the same man who had entered Navy flight training alongside her brother Henry Hooker Strong Jr., and whose shared cadet experience on A-4 Skyhawks had made him, in effect, family before the wedding. Herb Kinney went on to command a squadron aboard USS Kitty Hawk in 1972 — the same year her brother Henry was shot down over Vietnam — and later participated in the evacuation of Saigon in 1975 and the repatriation of the SS Mayaguez, rising to the rank of Captain and commanding USS Cleveland.

military_service.unit: VA-75, VA-52, USS Kitty Hawk, USS Cleveland
military_service.notes: Flew A-6 Intruders. Deployed USS Independence Vietnam 1965. Commanded VA-52 Oct 1972. Saigon evacuation April 1975; Mayaguez repatriation May 1975. Commanded USS Cleveland (LPD-7) 1978-80. JCS J-5 staff 1975.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his brother aviator's sister; flew to Saigon
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Kinney married Judith Strong on July 16, 1960 in Virginia Beach — the sister of his fellow A-4 Naval Aviation Cadet Henry Hooker Strong Jr., cementing a friendship made in cockpits into a family bond — and went on to one of the more operationally diverse naval careers of the Vietnam era: deploying aboard USS Independence to Vietnam in 1965, commanding the A-6 Intruder squadron VA-52 aboard USS Kitty Hawk in 1972, and participating in the evacuation of American nationals from Saigon in April 1975. He commanded USS Cleveland (LPD-7) from 1978 to 1980, served on the Joint Chiefs of Staff in the Politico/Military Affairs Directorate, and retired as a Captain — his brother-in-law Henry Hooker Strong Jr., his fellow cadet, having been shot down over North Vietnam in 1972, the same year Kinney assumed command of VA-52 in the same waters.

narrative_blocks.header: Evacuated Saigon; repatriated the SS Mayaguez
narrative_blocks.body: In April 1975, aboard USS Coral Sea, Charles Kinney participated in the evacuation of American nationals from Saigon as the city fell — one of the defining military operations at the close of American involvement in Vietnam — and the following month participated in the repatriation of the SS Mayaguez, the container ship seized by Cambodian forces whose recovery was one of the last American military actions of the Vietnam era. He had served in the Lebanon crisis of 1958, the Quemoy/Matsu crisis of 1958, and deployed multiple times to Southeast Asia and the Mediterranean — a naval career that traced the full arc of postwar American power projection.
